questions.prompt: The h-parameters are derived using which mathematical technique?
options: Two-variable Taylor series expansion around the Q-point
questions.correctAnswer: Two-variable Taylor series expansion around the Q-point
questions.explanation: The h-parameters come from expanding V_BE(I_B, V_CE) and I_C(I_B, V_CE) as two-variable Taylor series around the Q-point, keeping only first-order terms. Each h-parameter is a partial derivative evaluated at the operating point.

h_ie is the input impedance — the ratio of input voltage change to input current change, with the output voltage held constant. It has units of ohms and represents the resistance seen looking into the base.

questions.prompt: What element does h_fe·i_b represent in the equivalent circuit?
questions.explanation: h_fe·i_b is a current source whose magnitude depends on the input current i_b. It models the forward current gain of the BJT — the fact that a small base current controls a much larger collector current. This is the amplification mechanism.

questions.prompt: Why are the h-parameters called 'hybrid' parameters?
options: Because they combine NPN and PNP models
Because they mix different units (ohms, siemens, dimensionless)
Because they work for both AC and DC
Because they combine input and output characteristics
questions.correctAnswer: Because they mix different units (ohms, siemens, dimensionless)
questions.explanation: Unlike z-parameters (all in ohms) or y-parameters (all in siemens), h-parameters have mixed units: h_ie in Ω, h_oe in S, and h_fe and h_re are dimensionless ratios. This 'hybrid' nature gives them their name.

questions.prompt: The typical value of h_re for a common emitter BJT is about 2.5 × 10⁻⁴. This means:
options: The reverse feedback is very strong
A 10 V change in V_CE causes only 2.5 mV change in V_BE — often negligible
The BJT has no reverse feedback
The output impedance is very high
questions.correctAnswer: A 10 V change in V_CE causes only 2.5 mV change in V_BE — often negligible
questions.explanation: h_re = ΔV_BE/ΔV_CE ≈ 2.5×10⁻⁴, so a 10 V swing in V_CE only produces 2.5×10⁻⁴ × 10 = 2.5 mV at the input. This is so tiny that we usually set h_re ≈ 0 for CE analysis, removing the feedback source.

questions.prompt: For which configuration is h_r NOT negligible?
questions.explanation: For common collector, h_rc ≈ 1 — virtually all of the output voltage (V_EC) feeds back to the input. This makes sense physically because in CC (emitter follower), the output voltage closely tracks the input. For CE and CB, h_r ≈ 10⁻⁴ and can be safely neglected.

questions.prompt: The output element in the h-parameter model is:
options: A voltage source of value h_oe·v_ce
A current source of value h_oe·i_b
questions.explanation: h_oe is the output conductance (in siemens). In the equivalent circuit, it appears as a resistor of value 1/h_oe (converting conductance to resistance). For a typical CE BJT, h_oe ≈ 25 μA/V, so the resistance is 1/h_oe ≈ 40 kΩ.

questions.prompt: The second subscript 'e' in h_ie, h_re, h_fe, h_oe stands for:
questions.explanation: The second subscript identifies the BJT configuration: 'e' = common emitter, 'c' = common collector, 'b' = common base. The first subscript identifies the parameter type: i = input, r = reverse, f = forward, o = output.

questions.prompt: How is h_ie related to r_π in the hybrid-π model?
questions.explanation: The hybrid-π model's r_π is numerically equal to h_ie — both represent the small-signal input resistance looking into the base. The hybrid-π model is essentially the h-parameter model with h_re set to zero.

questions.prompt: If you are given CE h-parameters but the circuit is common base, you should:
options: Convert all h-parameters to CB values first
Insert the CE equivalent circuit and connect the terminals (B, C, E) appropriately
The analysis cannot be done without CB parameters
Use the CE parameters as if they were CB parameters
questions.correctAnswer: Insert the CE equivalent circuit and connect the terminals (B, C, E) appropriately
questions.explanation: Always use the model for which you have parameters. If given CE parameters, insert the CE equivalent circuit into your actual circuit, connecting base to base, collector to collector, and emitter to emitter. Then apply KVL/KCL. The circuit topology handles the configuration difference automatically.
